skill.name: chrome-devtools
skill.description: Use Chrome DevTools MCP from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code to inspect pages, automate browser actions, capture screenshots, and debug console or network behavior.
skill.summary: Automate Chrome DevTools MCP workflows for browser debugging, testing, screenshots, and page inspection.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, screenshot, and blocker patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows these are mostly documented Chrome DevTools MCP usage examples, but the skill still carries medium risk because it instructs agents to execute shell commands, download a latest remote package, control a browser, capture page content, and inspect network activity. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Runtime Execution of Remote Browser Tooling
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs the agent to run shell commands that install or execute chrome-devtools-mcp through package managers, including latest-version invocations. This is legitimate for a DevTools automation skill, but it creates supply-chain and command execution risk because code is fetched and run at task time.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and central to the skill workflow. They appear intended for legitimate browser debugging, but runtime package execution remains a real security risk.

medium_findings.title: Browser Data Exposure Through Screenshots, Snapshots, and Network Logs
medium_findings.description: The skill documents capabilities to capture screenshots, extract page snapshots, inspect console messages, inspect network requests, and run JavaScript in the browser. These operations can expose sensitive page content, session data, or request details if used on authenticated or private sites.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented tool list directly includes screenshot, snapshot, network inspection, and script evaluation actions. The skill itself warns that the server exposes full browser content.

medium_findings.title: Forceful Process Termination in Recommended Commands
medium_findings.description: Several quick-start and troubleshooting examples use a forceful process kill against chrome-devtools-mcp before starting a new browser automation session. This is a narrow operational risk, but it can terminate unrelated matching local work and should be used carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The kill command is explicit and repeated, but it targets a specific process pattern. The risk is disruption rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL and IP Examples Are Mostly Benign
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged hardcoded URLs and loopback IPs. Review shows they are examples such as localhost, example.com, httpbin.org, and 127.0.0.1 for browser debugging workflows, not evidence of covert exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples used to demonstrate navigation and local browser debugging. No evidence found that the skill sends collected data to those endpoints.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Link Triggered Filesystem Pattern
low_findings.description: The path traversal and hidden-file findings are caused by a relative link to a local documentation file. This is a repository navigation reference, not runtime filesystem traversal or hidden file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is a Markdown link to documentation. It is not used as executable input or a filesystem operation.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Matches Lack Malicious Context
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and screen-capture blocker patterns. Review found no cryptographic operation or reconnaissance payload; screenshot capture is an advertised browser debugging capability that requires user-directed page access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines describe browser automation documentation and known issues. No evidence found of malware-style cryptography, unauthorized reconnaissance, or automatic upload behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Pipeline Starts Browser Automation Server
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick-start pattern pipes commands into an MCP shell and launches chrome-devtools-mcp. This gives the agent practical browser control and should be limited to trusted targets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in the documented commands. It is useful for automation but materially expands what the agent can observe and control.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Debugging Connection Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill explains how to connect to an existing Chrome instance through a local debugging port. If used on a personal browser profile, this can expose active tabs and authenticated sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The browserUrl and remote-debugging-port examples are explicit. The risk depends on whether the user connects to an isolated browser or an existing sensitive session.

content.user_title: Debug Websites with Chrome DevTools MCP
content.value_statement: Browser issues often require console logs, network traces, screenshots, and DOM inspection. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Chrome DevTools MCP workflows for debugging and testing pages.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and launch the mcp CLI with chrome-devtools-mcp.
Provides commands for navigating pages and keeping a single browser session open.
Documents console log, network request, page snapshot, and screenshot workflows.
Shows form filling, clicking, waiting, page switching, and JavaScript evaluation patterns.
Includes troubleshooting steps for browser profile locks, hanging commands, and empty parameter schemas.
Explains isolated mode, headless mode, custom viewport settings, and existing Chrome connections.
limitations: Requires local command-line tools such as mcp, bunx, and Chrome-compatible browser support.
Runs external browser automation packages at task time unless users pin and verify versions.
Can expose sensitive browser content when used on authenticated or private pages.
Provides command recipes only; it does not include a packaged executable or custom source code.

use_cases.title: Debug a local web application
use_cases.description: Open localhost, collect console errors, inspect network requests, and capture the visible state for a failing page.

use_cases.title: Validate a browser workflow
use_cases.description: Navigate through forms, click controls, wait for visible text, and confirm that important page states appear.

use_cases.title: Investigate production page behavior
use_cases.description: Use snapshots, screenshots, and network logs to document a user-facing issue without manually switching tools.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Local Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open my local app in Chrome DevTools MCP, check console messages, and summarize any visible errors.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Page Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to the target page, capture a full-page screenshot, take a text snapshot, and describe the current UI state.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Network Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Load this page with Chrome DevTools MCP, inspect network requests, and identify failed or slow requests with likely causes.

prompt_templates.title: Automate a Debugging Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use one Chrome DevTools MCP session to open the page, perform the user flow, inspect console and network output, and report reproduction steps.

output_examples.input: Check why my local dashboard is not loading correctly.
output_examples.output: The report identifies console errors, failed requests, current page title, visible error text, and suggested next debugging steps.

output_examples.input: Take a screenshot and summarize the checkout page state.
output_examples.output: The response describes the captured page, notes visible form state, highlights validation messages, and lists any console warnings.

output_examples.input: Inspect network traffic while signing in to a test account.
output_examples.output: The result summarizes relevant requests, response status patterns, timing concerns, and privacy notes about captured session data.

best_practices: Use isolated browser profiles when testing pages that do not need personal sessions.
Pin and review external package versions before using the commands in sensitive environments.
Avoid screenshots, snapshots, and network logs on pages containing secrets or personal data.
anti_patterns: Do not connect DevTools MCP to a personal Chrome profile unless the session data is safe to inspect.
Do not run latest-version package commands in production or regulated environments without review.
Do not use browser automation against websites where testing or scraping is not authorized.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It provides Chrome DevTools MCP command workflows for browser debugging, testing, screenshots, snapshots, console logs, and network inspection.

faq.question: Does it include executable source code?
faq.answer: No. The skill is a Markdown instruction file with command examples for external MCP and browser tooling.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: It instructs agents to run shell commands, download remote packages, control Chrome, and collect browser page data.

faq.question: Can it inspect authenticated pages?
faq.answer: Yes, if connected to an authenticated browser session. Users should avoid exposing private pages or sensitive sessions.

faq.question: Which AI tools are listed as supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should users verify before use?
faq.answer: Users should verify package sources, use isolated browser profiles, and confirm they have permission to test each target site.
